text: The surgical team prepares for an extracorporeal Pringle maneuver by placing a tape around the hepatic hilum, secured with a chest tube through a 12mm trocar. The surgeon performs hilar dissection, isolating the right hepatic artery and portal vein, suspending them on tapes. Both vessels are temporarily clamped with bulldog clamps to identify the cantilever line. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to locate the lesion and mark the transection line with adequate margins. The bulldog clamps are released, and the artery and portal vein are divided using Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon isolates and controls accessory hepatic veins using clips or a stapler, depending on their size. The right lobe is fully immobilized, and the right hepatic vein is isolated, encircled, and divided with a vascular stapler. The parenchyma is divided using the Kelly crash technique with bipolar Maryland and harmonic devices. The right biliary duct is isolated within the parenchyma for safety and divided between Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon divides another branch of the right hepatic vein, completing the right hepatectomy. Attention is then shifted to the right colon, where the ileocolic vessels are ligated. The last ileal loop and transverse colon are stapled, and the specimen is collected in a laparoscopic bag. Preparation for a manual intracorporeal anastomosis follows, using a running V-lock suture.
